inputs.story: Julia has hired several new people to work as ride-managers for Walton World Amusement Park. She needs to check on their results after their first full day at work. Help her match each new employee to their ride and section of the park (broken down by color), as well as the number of people each served during that day. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett.
2. Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis.
3. Brett served more riders than Peter.
4. The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section.
5. Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section.
inputs.constants:
employees: Brett; Peter; Victor; Willis.
riders: 50 riders; 75 riders; 100 riders; 125 riders.
section: blue; green; orange; yellow.
inputs.solution:
Riders	Employees	Section
50 riders	Peter	blue
75 riders	Willis	orange
100 riders	Brett	yellow
125 riders	Victor	green

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
Julia has hired several new people to work as ride-managers for Walton World Amusement Park. She needs to check on their results after their first full day at work. Help her match each new employee to their ride and section of the park (broken down by color), as well as the number of people each served during that day. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett.
2. Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis.
3. Brett served more riders than Peter.
4. The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section.
5. Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section.

Constants:
employees: Brett; Peter; Victor; Willis.
riders: 50 riders; 75 riders; 100 riders; 125 riders.
section: blue; green; orange; yellow.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

ASP Program:
asp_generation.thinking:
Here's a thinking process:

1.  **Understand the Goal**: The user wants an ASP (Answer Set Programming) program in Clingo format that solves a specific logic grid puzzle. The program must follow the provided examples and rules strictly.

2.  **Analyze the Problem**:
   - **Story**: Julia hired new ride-managers at Walton World Amusement Park. Match each employee to their ride, section, and number of riders served.
   - **Categories & Constants**:
     - employees: Brett, Peter, Victor, Willis
     - riders: 50, 75, 100, 125 (strip "riders" from string)
     - section: blue, green, orange, yellow
   - **Clues**:
     1. The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett.
     2. Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis.
     3. Brett served more riders than Peter.
     4. The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section.
     5. Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section.
   - **Constraints**: One-to-one matching across all categories.

3.  **Map to ASP Concepts**:
   - **Categories**: `employee`, `riders`, `section`.
   - **Predicate**: `assign(E, R, S)` where E is employee, R is riders, S is section. (Or `match`, `assign`, etc. I'll use `assign` as in example 2, or `match` as in example 1. Let's stick to `match` or `assign`. The prompt says "Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch)." I'll use `match`.)
   - **Search Space**: `{match(E, R, S): riders(R), section(S)}=1 :- employee(E).`
   - **Uniqueness**: `{E1=E2; R1=R2; S1=S2}=0 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), (E1,R1,S1)!=(E2,R2,S2).`
   - **Clue Encoding**:
     1. `green` served more riders than `Brett`.
        Let `match(E1, R1, S1)` and `match(E2, R2, S2)`.
        `S1 = "green"`, `E2 = "Brett"`, `R1 > R2`.
        Constraint/Rule: `R1 > R2 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), S1="green", E2="Brett".`
     2. `Peter` served 25 fewer riders than `Willis`.
        `match(E1, R1, S1)` (Peter), `match(E2, R2, S2)` (Willis).
        `R1 = R2 - 25`.
        Rule: `R1 = R2 - 25 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), E1="Peter", E2="Willis".`
     3. `Brett` served more riders than `Peter`.
        `match(E1, R1, S1)` (Brett), `match(E2, R2, S2)` (Peter).
        `R1 > R2`.
        Rule: `R1 > R2 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), E1="Brett", E2="Peter".`
     4. `yellow` served 25 more riders than `orange`.
        `match(E1, R1, S1)` (yellow), `match(E2, R2, S2)` (orange).
        `R1 = R2 + 25`.
        Rule: `R1 = R2 + 25 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), S1="yellow", S2="orange".`
     5. `Peter` is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the `blue` section.
        `match(E, R, S)` where `E="Peter"`.
        `{R=125; S="blue"}=1`.
        Rule: `{R=125; S="blue"}=1 :- match(E,R,S), E="Peter".`

4.  **Check Consistency & Uniqueness**:
   - Let's manually solve to verify clues make sense and ASP will find a unique solution.
   - Employees: Brett, Peter, Victor, Willis
   - Riders: 50, 75, 100, 125
   - Sections: blue, green, orange, yellow
   - Clue 2: Peter = Willis - 25. Possible pairs (Willis, Peter): (75,50), (100,75), (125,100).
   - Clue 3: Brett > Peter.
   - Clue 1: Green > Brett.
   - So Green > Brett > Peter.
   - Since riders are 50, 75, 100, 125, and we need three distinct riders in descending order (Green, Brett, Peter), the only possible assignment is: Green=125, Brett=100, Peter=75.
   - Then Willis must be 100? Wait, Clue 2 says Peter = Willis - 25 => Willis = Peter + 25 = 75 + 25 = 100.
   - But Brett = 100. Contradiction? Wait, "no option in any category will ever be used more than once." So riders must be unique.
   - Let's re-read carefully: "Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis." => P = W - 25.
   - "Brett served more riders than Peter." => B > P.
   - "The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett." => G > B.
   - So G > B > P.
   - Riders available: 50, 75, 100, 125.
   - Possible triples for (G, B, P): (125, 100, 75) or (100, 75, 50).
   - If (G, B, P) = (125, 100, 75): P=75 => W = 75+25=100. But B=100. Conflict! Riders must be unique.
   - If (G, B, P) = (100, 75, 50): P=50 => W = 50+25=75. But B=75. Conflict!
   - Wait, did I misread clue 2? "Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis." => P = W - 25. Correct.
   - Maybe "riders" are not strictly numeric in the uniqueness constraint? The prompt says "riders: 50 riders; 75 riders; 100 riders; 125 riders." So they are numeric values.
   - Let's re-read clue 4: "The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section." => Y = O + 25.
   - So we have pairs with difference 25: (75,50), (100,75), (125,100).
   - Clue 2: W - P = 25.
   - Clue 4: Y - O = 25.
   - So Willis & Peter are a pair with diff 25. Yellow & Orange are a pair with diff 25.
   - Clue 1 & 3: G > B > P.
   - Let's list possible assignments for P: 50, 75, 100. (Cannot be 125 because G > B > P requires at least 3 distinct riders above P, but max is 125. If P=50, B=75, G=100 or 125. If P=75, B=100, G=125.)
   - Case A: P=50. Then W=75. B > 50. B could be 100 or 125.
     If B=100, G > 100 => G=125. Riders used: P=50, W=75, B=100, G=125. All distinct! Victor gets the remaining rider: 75? Wait, W=75, but B=100, G=125, P=50. Riders: 50, 75, 100, 125. All assigned. Victor must be the one with G=125? No, G is a section, not a person. The person in green section has 125 riders. So Victor could be in green.
     Let's check: P=50, W=75, B=100, G-section-person=125.
     Employees: Brett(100), Peter(50), Willis(75), Victor(125).
     Sections: Green=125 (Victor), Yellow & Orange pair diff 25. Remaining sections for B, P, W: Blue, Yellow, Orange.
     Clue 4: Y = O + 25. Riders for B(100), P(50), W(75). Pairs diff 25: (75,50) -> W and P. So W is in Yellow, P is in Orange. (Y=75, O=50).
     Then Brett(100) must be in Blue.
     Clue 5: Peter is either 125 or Blue. But Peter is 50 and Orange. Contradiction!
     So Case A fails Clue 5.

   - Case B: P=75. Then W=100. B > 75 => B=125. G > 125 => Impossible. Max rider is 125. So Case B impossible.
   - Wait, did I misread "green section served more riders than Brett"? Yes, G > B.
   - Maybe "riders" are not strictly ordered by value? No, they are numbers.
   - Let's re-read carefully: "The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett." => R(green) > R(Brett).
   - "Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis." => R(P) = R(W) - 25.
   - "Brett served more riders than Peter." => R(B) > R(P).
   - "The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section." => R(Y) = R(O) + 25.
   - "Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section."
   - Let's reconsider Case A: P=50, W=75, B=100, G-person=125.
     Clue 5 says P is 125 OR Blue. P is 50, so P must be Blue.
     So P is in Blue.
     Remaining sections for B(100), W(75), G-person(125): Green, Yellow, Orange.
     We know G-person has 125 riders. So Victor has 125 riders and is in Green.
     Remaining: B(100), W(75). Sections: Yellow, Orange.
     Clue 4: Y = O + 25. So Y=100, O=75. Thus B is Yellow, W is Orange.
     Check Clue 1: Green(125) > Brett(100). True.
     Check Clue 2: Peter(50) = Willis(75) - 25. True.
     Check Clue 3: Brett(100) > Peter(50). True.
     Check Clue 4: Yellow(100) = Orange(75) + 25. True.
     Check Clue 5: Peter is 125 or Blue. Peter is Blue. True.
     Solution:
     Brett: 100, Yellow
     Peter: 50, Blue
     Victor: 125, Green
     Willis: 75, Orange
     This works perfectly! My initial check failed because I assumed P must be Orange/Yellow based on diff 25, but P is Blue, so the diff 25 pair is B and W.

   - So the ASP encoding should correctly find this solution.

5.  **Draft ASP Code**:
   ```asp
   % Constants
   employee("Brett"; "Peter"; "Victor"; "Willis").
   riders(50; 75; 100; 125).
   section("blue"; "green"; "orange"; "yellow").

   % Search space
   {match(E, R, S): riders(R), section(S)}=1 :- employee(E).

   % Uniqueness
   {E1=E2; R1=R2; S1=S2}=0 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), (E1,R1,S1)!=(E2,R2,S2).

   % 1. The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett.
   R1 > R2 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), S1="green", E2="Brett".

   % 2. Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis.
   R1 = R2 - 25 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), E1="Peter", E2="Willis".

   % 3. Brett served more riders than Peter.
   R1 > R2 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), E1="Brett", E2="Peter".

   % 4. The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section.
   R1 = R2 + 25 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), S1="yellow", S2="orange".

   % 5. Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section.
   {R=125; S="blue"}=1 :- match(E,R,S), E="Peter".

   #show match/3.
   ```

6.  **Verify against Rules**:

   - Rule 2: Predicate `match/3` used. All categories appear.
   - Rule 3: Search space `{match(E, R, S): riders(R), section(S)}=1 :- employee(E).` Matches pattern.

     - Clue 3: `R1 > R2 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), E1="Brett", E2="Peter".` Correct.

asp_generation.response:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Brett"; "Peter"; "Victor"; "Willis").
riders(50; 75; 100; 125).
section("blue"; "green"; "orange"; "yellow").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one rider count R and section S.
{match(E, R, S): riders(R), section(S)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; R1=R2; S1=S2}=0 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), (E1,R1,S1)!=(E2,R2,S2).

% 1. The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett.
R1 > R2 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), S1="green", E2="Brett".

% 2. Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis.
R1 = R2 - 25 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), E1="Peter", E2="Willis".

% 3. Brett served more riders than Peter.
R1 > R2 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), E1="Brett", E2="Peter".

% 4. The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section.
R1 = R2 + 25 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), S1="yellow", S2="orange".

% 5. Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section.
{R=125; S="blue"}=1 :- match(E,R,S), E="Peter".

#show match/3.
```

initial_run.asp_program:
% Constants
employee("Brett"; "Peter"; "Victor"; "Willis").
riders(50; 75; 100; 125).
section("blue"; "green"; "orange"; "yellow").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one rider count R and section S.
{match(E, R, S): riders(R), section(S)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; R1=R2; S1=S2}=0 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), (E1,R1,S1)!=(E2,R2,S2).

% 1. The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett.
R1 > R2 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), S1="green", E2="Brett".

% 2. Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis.
R1 = R2 - 25 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), E1="Peter", E2="Willis".

% 3. Brett served more riders than Peter.
R1 > R2 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), E1="Brett", E2="Peter".

% 4. The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section.
R1 = R2 + 25 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), S1="yellow", S2="orange".

% 5. Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section.
{R=125; S="blue"}=1 :- match(E,R,S), E="Peter".

#show match/3.
